title: Noun Gender
summary: Every noun is either masculine or feminine — and adjectives must match.

sections.content: Unlike English, every Catalan noun has a grammatical gender: masculine or feminine. This affects the articles (the/a) you use before it, and the endings of any adjectives that describe it. There is no neutral 'it'.
Most nouns ending in -a are feminine. Most nouns ending in a consonant or -o are masculine. These are patterns, not absolute rules — there are exceptions.
Adjectives change their ending to agree with the noun they describe. A masculine adjective often ends in a consonant or -o; the feminine form typically adds or changes to -a.

items.catalan: un gat negre / una porta negra
items.english: a black cat / a black door

items.catalan: un cotxe vell / una taula vella
items.english: an old car / an old table

sections.content: Tip: always learn a noun together with its article (el / la). Don't memorise 'taula', memorise 'la taula'. The article encodes the gender.

practice.question: What is the gender of 'porta' (door)?
practice.explanation: 'Porta' ends in -a, which is the most common feminine ending. It takes 'la': la porta.

practice.question: Which article goes before 'cotxe' (car)?
practice.explanation: 'Cotxe' ends in -e and is masculine, so it takes 'el': el cotxe.

practice.question: A 'gat' (cat) is masculine. How do you say 'a small cat'?
practice.explanation: The adjective must be masculine to agree with 'gat'. 'Petit' is masculine; 'petita' is feminine.

practice.question: 'Pa' (bread) ends in -a. What gender is it?
practice.explanation: 'Pa' is an exception — it ends in -a but is masculine: el pa.
